candidate_problems.text: What, precisely, is a reflectively stable goal architecture for an embedded self-modifying agent, and can it be characterized formally enough to distinguish stable terminal goals from merely initially specifiable ones? This is the core unresolved issue because the conjecture hinges on a nontrivial notion of 'same goal over time' across ontology change, self-modification, and improved world-models.

Are the alleged structural pressures toward richer meta-goals, corrigibility, or conceptual stabilization genuinely necessary consequences of high capability under scarcity, or can there exist highly capable agents with arbitrary goals that remain stable without such meta-level elaboration? This tests whether the conjecture identifies a real constraint or overgeneralizes from particular agent designs.

What are the actual attractors in the space of intelligence-goal pairings under realistic dynamics—do they converge on a narrow class of survivable goal systems, and if so, by what mechanisms? This open question matters because the conjecture proposes a systems-level selection effect but does not yet explain the structure, breadth, or empirical detectability of those attractors.
